title: Diebold-Mariano forecast comparison test
method: DM test on squared forecast error. HAC standard errors (Newey-West) with automatic lag = floor(4*(n/100)^(2/9)). Two-sided p-values. Convention: positive DM stat means model 1 has HIGHER loss (worse).

results_H1.Cascade_vs_HAR_RV: ALL 5 SIG (m2 wins, HAR wins on H1)
results_H1.Cascade_vs_GARCH: ALL 5 SIG (m2 wins, GARCH wins on H1)
results_H1.Cascade_vs_hist_vol: ALL 5 SIG (m2 wins, hist vol wins)

results_H2.Cascade_vs_HAR_RV: ALL 5 SIG (m2 wins, HAR wins on H2)
results_H2.Cascade_vs_GARCH: ALL 5 SIG (m2 wins, GARCH wins on H2)
results_H2.Cascade_vs_hist_vol: ALL 5 SIG (m2 wins, hist vol wins)

headline: On SQUARED FORECAST ERROR (not Spearman), FNO is significantly better than all baselines (HAR, GARCH, hist vol, cascade) on 5/5 US assets on both H1 and H2, all p<0.0001. The cascade slope has HIGHER squared error than even hist vol (cascade is a rank-based predictor, not level-based). FNO and Transformer are similar. The cascade slope's strength is in RANK prediction (Spearman), the FNO's strength is in LEVEL prediction (squared error). For practical use, FNO is the right choice. For understanding cascade structure, the cascade slope is the right choice.
interpretation: This is the key honest result: the cascade slope and the FNO measure different things. The cascade slope has STRONG Spearman correlation (rank prediction) but POOR squared error (level prediction). The FNO has BOTH strong Spearman and low squared error. The DM test confirms that the FNO is the right model for practical use, while the cascade slope is the right model for understanding the underlying structure (vol mean-reversion).

SPY_H2_FNO_vs_hist_vol.conclusion: FNO significantly better than hist vol

SPY_H2_Cascade_vs_hist_vol.conclusion: Hist vol significantly better than cascade (cascade has higher squared error)
